title: Marriage
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Marriage
summary: Scripture presents marriage as a covenant of mutual self-giving, rooted in creation's design and elevated by the New Testament as a reflection of Christ's love for the church.
body:
Marriage in Scripture begins not with a command but with a recognition. God saw that it was not good for the man to be alone and created a partner corresponding to him [verse:gen.2.18]. The result is a one-flesh union — a bond so deep that Jesus later cited it as the reason divorce was never part of the original design [verse:matt.19.4-6].

The most extensive New Testament teaching on marriage appears in Ephesians 5, where Paul frames the husband-wife relationship as a living picture of Christ and the church [verse:eph.5.25-33]. The standard for husbands is not authority but sacrifice — loving as Christ loved, which means giving himself up. Wives are called to respect, and the whole passage is prefaced by mutual submission [verse:eph.5.21].

Proverbs offers practical wisdom: a spouse of noble character is worth more than rubies [verse:prov.31.10]. The Song of Solomon celebrates the passionate, sensual dimension of marital love without embarrassment — desire within covenant is a gift, not a concession [verse:song.8.6-7].

Paul also addresses the challenges honestly. Marriage brings divided attention and legitimate concern for a spouse's needs [verse:1cor.7.32-34]. It is not an escape from difficulty but a context for sacrificial love.

Scripture treats marriage as both beautiful and demanding — a covenant that images something far larger than itself.

verses.annotation: God identifies human solitude as the first 'not good' and creates partnership.
Jesus roots marriage in creation's design: what God has joined, let no one separate.
Marriage as a reflection of Christ's self-giving love for the church.
The often-missed preface: submit to one another out of reverence for Christ.
A noble spouse's worth exceeds material wealth.
Love is as strong as death — passionate marital love celebrated without apology.
Paul's honest acknowledgment that marriage brings real complexity and divided attention.

scholars.quote: Sarna explains that the 'one flesh' language in Genesis 2:24 describes not merely physical union but the establishment of a new primary kinship bond that supersedes even the parent-child relationship.

Fee argues that Paul's teaching on marriage in 1 Corinthians 7 is remarkably egalitarian for its era — both husband and wife have mutual authority over each other's bodies, a reciprocity that was counter-cultural in the ancient world.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 1987)

scholars.quote: Longman reads the Song of Solomon as a celebration of erotic love within covenant — the book's inclusion in Scripture is a theological affirmation that physical desire between spouses is part of God's good design.
